schools.summary: Henry contrasts the restraining providence of God with the weakness of Saul and the piety of Jonathan, 'a sweet imp out of a crab-stock.'
Keil, Ellicott, and Clarke fix the topography and read Jonathan as the type of the faith-filled Hebrew hero that Saul conspicuously lacked.
Gill dates the enterprise to the night before, citing Josephus that Jonathan reached the camp by daybreak.

themes: a son fitter than his father

schools.summary: Gill reads Saul entrenched in the strongest, furthest part of Gibeah, at greatest distance from the enemy.
Keil identifies the priest Ahiah with the later Ahimelech; Ellicott keeps 'pomegranate tree' and notes Saul's fondness for trees.
Wesley places Saul in the outworks, watching the Philistines; Clarke reads Rimmon as the strong rock of the fugitive Benjamites.

debates.question: Is 'Rimmon' the pomegranate tree or the rock of refuge?

sides.position: a literal pomegranate tree, in keeping with Saul's known love of trees
the strong rock Rimmon where Benjamites once sheltered, possibly the same cavern

schools.summary: Gill carefully disentangles the genealogy, making Ahiah the son of Ahitub rather than of Ichabod.
Clarke and Wesley identify Ahiah with Ahimelech and stress that the ephod is the high-priestly garment bearing the Urim and Thummim.
Ellicott reads the name as a memorial of the fall of Eli's house, and defines the ephod as the oracular vestment.

themes: the house of Eli under judgment

schools.summary: Gill maps the two passes held by the garrison, stressing the greatness of Jonathan's difficulties.
Wesley corrects the geography — the crags faced north and south, forcing Jonathan to climb; Clarke gives the Targum's meanings 'slippery' and 'treading down.'
Keil cites Robinson's survey of the Wady Suweinit; Ellicott notes the disputed antiquity of the names Bozez and Seneh.

debates.question: What do the names Bozez and Seneh mean?

sides.position: 'slippery' and 'treading down' (Targum)
perhaps 'shining' and 'the acacia'

schools.summary: Gill fixes the plain compass points — one crag toward Michmash and the enemy, one toward Gibeah and Saul.
Keil reads the surveyed ground into Jonathan's conviction that Israel is the nation of the omnipotent God who saves those who wholly trust Him.

themes: Israel as the nation of God

schools.summary: Wesley balances Jonathan's covenant confidence against the humble 'it may be' — sure of God, unsure of the time and manner; Clarke calls it 'a fine sentiment.'
Ellicott argues the Spirit of the Lord came upon Jonathan though unnamed, and quotes Bishop Hall: the question is not what Jonathan can do but what God can do.
Gill notes Jonathan repeats and enlarges his call to encourage the frightened armour-bearer.

themes: no restraint to save by many or few

schools.summary: Clarke reads the armour-bearer's pledge to share every danger as unreserved loyalty.
Ellicott treasures the preserved colloquial words 'Go on; I will follow'; Keil makes the servant's assent the hinge to Jonathan's seeking a sign.
Gill notes the young man answered 'readily and cheerfully.'
Gill marvels at the daring of an open daylight crossing over sheer crags to an entrenched foe.
Keil judges Jonathan's proposed sign 'well chosen' and denies it was tempting God, since he acted in his calling for the kingdom of God in confident faith.

themes: seeking God's leading vs tempting God

schools.summary: Gill reads the token as the means by which the two would know both their conduct and the outcome.
Keil explains the sign as a shrewd test of the enemy's courage — a cry to 'tarry' would show boldness, a taunt to 'come up' would show cowardice.
Clarke likens Jonathan's request to Eliezer's sign at the well, a faith-appeal to the God who governs even unbelievers' words.

themes: the negative half of the sign

schools.summary: Gill reads the taunt 'come up' as betraying the enemy's fear and providing the very opening Jonathan needs.
Wesley grounds the sign in God's government of the hearts and tongues of all men, even those who know Him not.

themes: the positive half of the sign

schools.summary: Gill notes the two crossed the valley and stood in open view, easily known as Hebrews.
Ellicott observes the garrison could have crushed them with rolled stones but disarmed itself through contempt.
Henry hears in the sneer a warning: when some of Christ's soldiers play the coward, the brave are reproached for it.

themes: the taunt as the awaited sign

schools.summary: Wesley and Clarke read the taunt as the requested sign, and note Jonathan piously ascribes victory to God and to 'the hand of Israel,' not his own.
Henry marks the growth from 'it may be' (v.6) to 'the LORD hath delivered' — humble faith ripened by God's answer into full assurance.
Keil confirms the taunt gave Jonathan the token he sought.
Gill pictures the guards descrying and challenging the pair.

themes: glory to God, good to Israel

schools.summary: Gill explains Jonathan climbed an inaccessible precipice on hands and feet, taking the garrison unawares.
Ellicott and Stanley picture the paralysed guards and the archer-prince shooting his bolts in rapid succession.
Wesley and Clarke divide the labour of the two men and trace the collapse to a God-sent panic that turned the Philistines' swords on each other.
Gill reads the tally as ferocious speed — twenty killed in a very little time and a very small space.
Ellicott surveys the obscure text — the LXX's 'darts and slings,' Ewald's ploughing simile, Bunsen's poetic fragment — and prefers twenty felled within half a rood.
Clarke explains the ancient land-measure as a proverb for a very small space.

debates.question: What does the strange half-acre measure describe?

sides.position: twenty men felled one after another within half a rood of ground
a poetic or conjectural phrase (LXX 'slings and stones'; a fragment of a poet)

themes: twenty men in half an acre

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott read a God-infused supernatural terror; Ellicott insists the earthquake is literal, ranking it with the Red Sea, Beth-horon, and Kishon.
Wesley expounds 'a trembling of God' as supernatural terror from God's own hand; Clarke prefers to read the earthquake as metaphor for great commotion.
Henry stresses there was no visible cause for fear, so the dread must be God's — He can make the proud afraid of a shadow.

debates.question: Was the earthquake literal or figurative?

sides.position: a real earthquake, nature summoned as at the Red Sea and Beth-horon
probably a metaphor for great commotion, though God could have sent a real one

schools.summary: Gill sets the watchmen on Gibeah's height, able in daylight to see the host dissolving.
Keil and Ellicott wrestle with the obscure Hebrew, preferring the Rabbinic 'more and more broken up.'
Clarke cites the Vulgate's 'the multitude were prostrate,' many slain by Jonathan and by each other.
Henry sees God setting the Philistines against one another, their monopoly of arms turned to their own destruction, as with Gideon and Jehoshaphat.
Keil and Ellicott read Saul rightly guessing an Israelite attack and mustering to find Jonathan and his armour-bearer missing.
Clarke sees the muster as Saul's attempt to learn who had begun the rout.
Gill pictures Saul addressing the muster-master among his officers.
Keil and Ellicott argue the LXX's 'ephod' is original, since inquiry was made by the Urim in the ephod, not by the far-off ark; the Hebrew 'ark' clause is likely a scribal gloss.
Gill, with Kimchi, reads Saul calling for the priest to inquire by Urim and Thummim.
Clarke reads Saul simply wishing to inquire how to use the favourable moment.

debates.question: Did Saul call for the ark or the ephod?

sides.position: the ephod (LXX, Josephus) — the oracular vestment with Urim and Thummim
the ark itself, for encouragement at a moment of danger (MT)

schools.summary: Keil gives the literal 'draw thy hand back'; Ellicott, quoting Andrewes and Hall, sees the general silencing the priest — the ark giving place to arms.
Henry makes it a proverb: he that believeth will not make such haste as to refuse time to take God with him.
Wesley and Clarke read Saul, discerning the moment, breaking off the inquiry to seize the hour.

themes: he that believeth will not make haste

schools.summary: Ellicott reads Israel raising the Benjamite war-cry and driving wedge-like into the host; Keil traces the mutual slaughter to terror and the turning auxiliaries.
Gill weighs whether the Gibeah thousand rejoined Saul's six hundred.
Henry makes the rout a figure of Christ's finished victory, which the church is only called to pursue and to spoil.

themes: every man's sword against his fellow

schools.summary: Gill canvasses whether the returning Hebrews were bondsmen, conscripts, or deserters gone over to the stronger side.
Keil distinguishes 'Hebrews' (the foreign name) from 'Israel' (the sacred name) and supplies 'returned'; Ellicott sees compelled prisoners or Philistine levies.
Clarke notes the LXX read 'slaves'; Wesley says they had joined the enemy by constraint or policy and now returned.
Gill recalls these men had hidden in caves and pits in the earlier terror of chapter 13.
Keil traces the pursuit beyond Bethaven toward Ajalon; Clarke notes the LXX/Vulgate addition of ten thousand men.
Henry weighs the late-comers — no praise for joining only once victory is sure, yet greater reproach had they stayed away.
Gill marvels that two men routed a vast army — a free, unmerited favour to an undeserving prince.
Ellicott hears the Red Sea formula and dates the crushing of Philistine power to this decisive victory.
Wesley: God saved by the warriors, for without Him they could do nothing — salvation is of the Lord.
Henry echoes that God did it by them; the human instruments could do nothing apart from Him.
Keil condemns the oath as false zeal and a despotic measure, betrayed by 'avenge myself upon mine enemies.'
Henry calls it unwise, imperious, and impious — 'bless, and curse not' — and suspects Saul is the cursing enemy of Psalm 109.
Clarke notes the fast defeated its own aim; Wesley grants Saul's good intention but faults its rigour and its accursed penalty.
Gill agrees the order would have passed but for the curse annexed to it.

debates.question: Was Saul's oath merely rash, or well-intentioned?

sides.position: false zeal and despotism, self-regarding and cruel
a good intention marred only by excessive rigour and its curse

schools.summary: Gill limits 'all they of the land' to those pursuing with Saul and Jonathan.
Keil describes the woody district; Ellicott and Clarke naturalize the wild honey of a land flowing with milk and honey.
Keil and Ellicott render 'a stream of honey'; Clarke compares honey-dews he has seen on trees and grass.
Gill traces the honey to trees, combs, or hives crushed by the fleeing Philistines.
Henry commends the men's self-denial — true Israelites deny their appetite for fear of a curse — while the narrative still faults the oath.
Wesley notes bees nesting in trees, rocks, and holes, eminently in Canaan.

themes: a good instinct under a bad command

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil read the enlightened eyes as strength restored by the honey, and Ellicott cites the Talmud (Yoma) making it a rule of health.
Clarke and Wesley explain that hunger dims sight and food clears it — Jonathan was simply refreshed.
Gill stresses Jonathan innocently heard nothing of the oath, being off in the fighting.
Ellicott renders 'the people are faint' as the soldier's own complaint; Keil records Jonathan at once condemning the prohibition.
Gill pictures Jonathan encouraging the men to eat when a soldier overhears and reports the oath, clearing Jonathan of willful disobedience.

themes: the father's word vs the son's instinct

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil endorse Jonathan's complaint on the merits — the ill-considered vow cost Israel a fuller victory.
Gill grants the truth of the charge but faults Jonathan for a son publicly reflecting on his father, risking mutiny.

debates.question: Was Jonathan right to say his father had troubled the land?

sides.position: a true and justified naming of real harm
true in substance but unwise and unbecoming in a son, tending to sedition

themes: my father hath troubled the land

schools.summary: Gill draws out Jonathan's argument from the lesser to the greater — if a little honey revived him, a full meal from the spoil would have carried the army through.
Henry agrees: bread strengthens the heart, and God gives daily bread for daily work; withholding it in the name of devotion is folly.

themes: the greater slaughter that never was

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil identify Aijalon as Yalo and gauge the pursuit's distance; Keil names Saul's fast as the cause of the faintness.
Gill, following Bunting, reckons Aijalon twelve miles from Michmash.
Clarke gives the distance as three or four leagues.
Ellicott quotes Stanley on the Ramadan-like rush at sunset and cites the blood laws of Leviticus; Keil confirms from the Hebrew that the beasts were eaten upon their blood.
Wesley marks the irony — those who feared the king's curse make no scruple of breaking God's command; Clarke calls it another bad fruit of Saul's oath.
Gill pictures the famished people flying like ravenous birds upon the spoil.

themes: the fear of man over the fear of God

schools.summary: Keil renders Saul's charge 'ye act faithlessly' and reads the stone as a place to drain the blood; Ellicott makes Saul's letter-scruple the key to his complex character.
Wesley exposes the blind spot — Saul sees the people's fault but not his own in causing it.

debates.question: How should Saul's sudden zeal for the blood-law be judged?

sides.position: a genuinely right instinct to halt the army and honour the Law
scrupulous over the letter yet blind to his own guilt in causing the sin

themes: sees their fault, not his own
the child of his own oath

schools.summary: Gill pictures Saul's heralds dispersing to publish his order through the camp.
Keil explains the beasts were slain at the stone so the blood drained and the flesh was separated from it.
Henry draws the maxim: the people were easily reformed once the prince did his part — good government restrains evil with more ease than is imagined.
Ellicott surveys Abarbanel, the Midrash, and the plain sense — Saul's first public acknowledgment of God; Keil reads a memorial altar rather than a place of sacrifice.
Clarke sees Saul again presuming to act the prophet's part, building without Samuel as he once sacrificed without him.
Henry hears hollow piety — zeal for the form of godliness while denying its power, like Israel building temples having forgotten its Maker.

debates.question: Was Saul's first altar true devotion or hollow form?

sides.position: a genuine thanksgiving and memorial of God's mercy
the reflex of a hollow piety, zealous for forms while denying the power

themes: the form vs the power of godliness

schools.summary: Henry sees zeal without discretion in Saul and commends the priest as the remembrancer who takes God along with the king.
Ellicott makes Ahiah the lone brave voice against wholesale bloodshed; Keil and Clarke note the priest sought God's decision before pursuing.
Keil and Ellicott read the silent oracle as signalling some public guilt requiring atonement before God would answer.
Henry makes it doctrine: unanswered prayer calls us to find and put away the sin that separates us from God.
Clarke dissents — Jonathan's honey was no sin; the silence rebuked Saul's vindictive night-pursuit and the people's uncleanness in eating blood.

debates.question: Why did God not answer Saul that day?

sides.position: to point providentially to a transgression the lot would expose
because Saul's night-pursuit was vindictive and the people were defiled by the blood

schools.summary: Gill weighs 'the corners of the peoples' as the princes, cornerstones of the nation, and notes only a small number were present.
Ellicott and Keil confirm pinnoth as 'corner stones' and picture Saul summoning the chiefs by night around the altar to find the transgressor.

themes: earnest to purge others, blind to himself

schools.summary: Keil renders the emphatic 'even upon my son he shall die,' the people silent from terror; Ellicott, with Ewald, hears Saul bound by his oath amid a horror-stricken hush.
Wesley reads the silence as love — none would inform against Jonathan, whose ignorance excused him, over so small an offence.
Gill marks the oath sworn by 'the LORD which saveth Israel,' invoked against Israel's own saviour.

themes: though it be in Jonathan, he shall die
the vow at war with itself

schools.summary: Gill notes the summons now reaches the whole army, common soldiers included, who again defer to Saul's pleasure.
Henry reads Saul's placing himself and Jonathan against Israel as confidence in their innocence, and commends the appearance of impartial justice even as it drives toward the son.
Ellicott and Keil discuss 'give a perfect lot' / 'show the innocent' and the long LXX and Vulgate expansions of Saul's prayer; Clarke gives the fuller Latin form.
Wesley reads the prayer as asking God to declare the guiltless and guide the lot.
Henry makes lots a solemn prayerful appeal to Providence, as in Acts 1:24 — and here God's answer falls on Saul's own house.

themes: the lot as appeal to Providence

schools.summary: Wesley and Clarke insist the lot named Jonathan as the actor without pronouncing him guilty — God ordered it to chastise Saul's folly and clear Jonathan's innocence.
Gill reads Saul's inclusion of himself as a show of strict, impartial justice.

themes: God's design to chasten the father

schools.summary: Keil marks Jonathan's quiet resignation — he confesses the honey and yields to the sentence.
Henry praises Jonathan's great and generous mind — as brave to yield to death as to storm the cliff, saying in effect 'God's and my father's will be done.'
Gill notes Saul demanded to know the sin the lot had exposed.

themes: a little honey, and I must die

schools.summary: Clarke denounces the sentence as saving a rash oath, likening it to Herod beheading John the Baptist — 'unfeeling brute.'
Henry faults the hasty, furious sentence and warns the self-curse recoiled on Saul, who was himself rejected and made anathema.
Keil notes the 'God do so and more also' formula, a binding self-imprecation as in Ruth 1:17.
Gill records the oath-formula and Abarbanel's attempt to soften it to an asseveration.
Keil finds the people's objection conclusive — Jonathan's act was wrong only by an oath he never heard, so Saul was obliged to yield.
Clarke frames a righteous jury acquitting Jonathan; Wesley reads 'wrought with God' as God's own owning of him in the day's work.
Henry celebrates the people opposing oath to oath, rescuing Jonathan by reason and prayer, zealous for God's chosen instrument.
Keil and Ellicott read Saul, conscious now that the fault was his, breaking off the pursuit and acquiescing in Ahiah's reluctance.
Gill notes Saul returned home for lack of an answer, the opportunity lost in examining Jonathan.
Henry laments the quashed design — when Israel's shields clash, the public service suffers, and the victory's fullness was forfeited.

themes: division costs the people of God

schools.summary: Keil reads Saul now truly securing the kingship; Ellicott takes the verse as a general heading to a reign of perpetual wars and surveys the disputed 'he vexed them.'
Gill, with Kimchi and Abarbanel, reads Saul recovered and strengthened in the kingdom, now assuming the majesty of a king.
Henry sums it — Saul guarded his country on every side and vexed its enemies wherever he turned.

debates.question: Does 'took the kingdom' mark this victory or head the whole reign?

sides.position: here Saul first truly secured the kingship over Israel
a general heading to the list of wars waged from the reign's beginning

themes: a flawed king established by God

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil prefer 'he wrought mightily' and note the Amalek war is singled out as prominent, to be told fully in the next chapter with grave consequences for Saul's sovereignty.
Clarke notes the war is recounted in the following chapter.
Keil identifies Ishui with Abinadab and lists the three sons who fell at Gilboa; Ellicott explains Merab and Michal are named for their part in David's history.
Wesley notes Ishui is Abinadab and Ish-bosheth is omitted as one who did not share these battles; Clarke queries why the daughters are then named.

themes: the sons and daughters of Saul

schools.summary: Keil clarifies Abner as Saul's cousin and reads 'sons of Abiel'; Ellicott praises Abner's rare ability and notes David's lament over him.
Gill takes Ahinoam as mother of Saul's children and notes no second wife is recorded.

themes: names that seed the coming sorrow

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil read 'sons of Abiel,' harmonizing the genealogy with 1 Chronicles 9:35 (Jehiel) and 1 Samuel 9:1, and fixing Saul and Abner as kinsmen.

themes: Kish and Ner, sons of Abiel
a royal house of one clan

schools.summary: Keil ties the lifelong Philistine war to Saul's recruiting; Ellicott sees a nascent standing army and compares Saul's eye for strong men to Frederick William of Prussia.
Gill notes the Philistines recovered and troubled Saul to the end, when he died in battle with them.
Henry closes with pathos — Saul had little enjoyment of his crown; earthly glory blazes just before the dark night of woe, fulfilling Samuel's warning that the king would take their sons.
